subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Every single day, from the moment you wake before Fajr to the moment you sleep at night, your body is quietly working. It is building new cells, repairing tissue, fighting illness, and giving you the energy to walk to school, help at home, play football, and think clearly in class. Where does all that energy and building material come from? It comes from the food you eat. In this unit you will discover the amazing journey that a plate of bariis (rice) with fish, or a piece of canjeero with a cup of camel milk, takes as it travels through your body.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured directly from the Stage 8 science workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Name the main food groups and nutrients - carbohydrates, proteins, fats (lipids), vitamins, minerals, water and fibre - and give a local food example of each.
Explain the job of each nutrient in the body and describe what happens when a nutrient is missing (deficiency).
Describe a balanced diet and explain why different people (a farmer, a baby, a pregnant mother, a person fasting) need different amounts of food.
Name the organs of the digestive system in order and state the function of each: mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, and the helper organs (liver, pancreas).
Explain the difference between mechanical and chemical digestion and give an example of each.
Describe what enzymes are and explain how they break large food molecules into small, soluble ones.
Explain absorption - how digested food passes into the blood through the wall of the small intestine.
Carry out simple food tests for starch, sugar and fat, and interpret the results.
Apply your knowledge to plan a healthy, balanced meal from foods available in your community.

concepts.title: You already know that living things need food - you learned…
concepts.explanation: You already know that living things need food - you learned this when you studied the seven life processes and the characteristics of living organisms. Now you will look much more closely. You will learn what different foods actually contain, why your body needs each type of nutrient, and how your digestive system breaks down the food on your plate into tiny particles small enough to enter your blood. You will meet the idea of an enzyme, one of the most important discoveries in biology, and you will understand the…
concepts.example: This is not just science for an exam. It is science for life. When you understand digestion and a balanced diet, you can make wiser choices: choosing dates and fruit instead of too many sugary sweets, drinking clean…

concepts.title: This is not just science for an exam. It is science for…
concepts.explanation: This is not just science for an exam. It is science for life. When you understand digestion and a balanced diet, you can make wiser choices: choosing dates and fruit instead of too many sugary sweets, drinking clean water, washing your hands before eating as our faith and good hygiene both teach, and eating well at suhoor so that you have strength to fast during Ramadan. A healthy body is a trust (amaanah) given to you, and caring for it is part of being a thoughtful young person.
concepts.example: [AI] Ask Your AI Tutor Tell your AI tutor what you ate for your last meal. Ask: 'Which food groups were in my meal, and which nutrient was missing?' Then ask it to suggest one small change to make the meal more balanced.

explorations.title: Testing Foods For Starch
explorations.context: To find out which local foods contain starch, using iodine solution as the test.
explorations.prompt: Before you begin, write down which foods you predict will contain starch and which will not. For example: 'I think rice and canjeero will contain starch, but oil and sugar will not.' Give a reason.
explorations.answer: Which foods contained starch? Were your predictions correct? Starch is a type of which nutrient group? Why do these foods give us energy? Rice and canjeero both turned blue-black. What does this tell you they have in common?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out which local foods contain starch, using iodine solution as the test.

explorations.title: The Fat Test With Paper
explorations.context: To find out which foods contain fat (lipid), using the grease-spot test on paper.
explorations.prompt: Predict which foods will leave a greasy mark on paper. Write your prediction and a reason.
explorations.answer: Why did we include a drop of water as a comparison (a control)? Which foods left a permanent greasy spot? What nutrient do they contain? Fat gives more energy per gram than carbohydrate. Why might a small amount of oil or groundnuts be useful for someone doing heavy work?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out which foods contain fat (lipid), using the grease-spot test on paper.

explorations.title: Modelling Mechanical Digestion And Surface Area
explorations.context: To show why chewing (mechanical digestion) speeds up chemical digestion, by seeing how surface area affects how fast something dissolves.
explorations.prompt: Predict: will a whole lump of sugar or the same amount of crushed sugar dissolve faster in water? Give your reason.
explorations.answer: Which dissolved faster - the whole lump or the crushed sugar? Why? The crushed sugar has a larger total surface area touching the water. How does this connect to why we chew our food? Explain how this experiment models the teamwork between mechanical and chemical digestion in your mouth.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To show why chewing (mechanical digestion) speeds up chemical digestion, by seeing how surface area affects how fast something dissolves.

explorations.title: Planning And Investigating A Balanced Plate
explorations.context: To investigate the meals eaten in your home for one day and design a balanced plate.
explorations.prompt: Predict which nutrient your family's meals will have the most of, and which they may lack.
explorations.answer: Which nutrient appeared most often in your family's day? Which appeared least? Was the diet balanced overall? Explain your answer. Suggest one cheap, local food that would improve the balance, and say which nutrient it adds.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To investigate the meals eaten in your home for one day and design a balanced plate.

methods.title: Testing Foods For Starch
methods.example: To find out which local foods contain starch, using iodine solution as the test.
steps: Wash your hands and lay out a small piece of each food in its own spot on the plate.
Add one or two drops of iodine solution onto each food.
Watch carefully for about 30 seconds. A blue-black colour means starch is present. If the iodine stays its normal brown-orange colour, there is no starch.
Record the colour for each food in the table below.
Observation Table

methods.title: The Fat Test With Paper
methods.example: To find out which foods contain fat (lipid), using the grease-spot test on paper.
steps: Draw and label a small circle on the paper for each food.
Rub a little of each food onto its circle. For water, put one drop.
Leave the paper in a warm, airy place for about 15 minutes so any water dries.
Hold the paper up to the light. A translucent (see-through) greasy spot that does NOT dry away means the food contains fat. The water spot will dry and leave no permanent mark.
Record your results.

methods.title: Modelling Mechanical Digestion And Surface Area
methods.example: To show why chewing (mechanical digestion) speeds up chemical digestion, by seeing how surface area affects how fast something dissolves.
steps: Fill both glasses with the same amount of water at the same temperature.
Into glass A, add the whole lump of sugar. Into glass B, add the crushed sugar. Do not stir.
Watch and time how long each takes to dissolve completely.
Record the times.
Observation Table

methods.title: Planning And Investigating A Balanced Plate
methods.example: To investigate the meals eaten in your home for one day and design a balanced plate.
steps: For one day, write down every food eaten at each meal in your home.
For each food, decide which nutrient group(s) it mainly provides.
Fill in the record sheet below, ticking which nutrients appeared.
Look for any nutrient group that was missing or eaten very little.
Design an improved balanced plate that fills the gap using local, affordable foods.

workedExamples.title: Planning A Balanced Plate
workedExamples.prompt: Let us plan a balanced lunch for Maryam, a 12-year-old who walks to school and plays outside. We will check that every nutrient group is included.
workedExamples.solution: Step 1 - Start with energy (carbohydrate). Maryam is active, so she needs good energy. We choose a portion of bariis (rice). This supplies starch for steady energy. Step 2 - Add growth food (protein). She is still growing, so she needs protein. We add a piece of grilled fish from the coast, or a serving of beans if fish is not available. Step 3 - Add vitamins, minerals and fibre. We add a spoonful of cooked vegetables (such as spinach or carrots) and a fresh mango for vitamin C and fibre. Step 4 - Include a little…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: Which food is the best source of protein for growth? (a) rice (b) cooking oil (c) fish (d) sugar
workedExamples.solution: 2 (c) fish - fish is rich in protein for growth and repair; the others are mainly energy or empty.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: Iodine solution is used to test for: (a) fat (b) sugar (c) protein (d) starch
workedExamples.solution: 3 (d) starch - iodine is the standard test for starch.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: A blue-black colour with iodine shows the presence of: (a) fat (b) starch (c) water (d) vitamin C
workedExamples.solution: 4 (b) starch - the blue-black colour is the positive starch result.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: Which enzyme breaks down starch? (a) protease (b) lipase (c) amylase (d) bile
workedExamples.solution: 5 (c) amylase - amylase (a carbohydrase) breaks starch into sugar.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Where does most absorption of digested food take place? (a) mouth (b) stomach (c) small intestine (d) large intestine
workedExamples.solution: 6 (c) small intestine - its villi absorb most digested food into the blood.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: The wave-like squeezing that pushes food along the gut is called: (a) absorption (b) peristalsis (c) digestion (d) respiration
workedExamples.solution: 7 (b) peristalsis - muscular waves push food along the gut.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: The main job of the large intestine is to: (a) digest protein (b) absorb most water (c) make bile (d) chew food
workedExamples.solution: 8 (b) absorb most water - the large intestine reabsorbs water, leaving solid waste.

practice.prompt: Which nutrient is the body's main and fastest source of energy? (a) protein (b) carbohydrate (c) vitamin (d) fibre
practice.answer: 1 (b) carbohydrate - carbohydrates give the fastest, main energy supply.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which food is the best source of protein for growth? (a) rice (b) cooking oil (c) fish (d) sugar
practice.answer: 2 (c) fish - fish is rich in protein for growth and repair; the others are mainly energy or empty.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Iodine solution is used to test for: (a) fat (b) sugar (c) protein (d) starch
practice.answer: 3 (d) starch - iodine is the standard test for starch.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A blue-black colour with iodine shows the presence of: (a) fat (b) starch (c) water (d) vitamin C
practice.answer: 4 (b) starch - the blue-black colour is the positive starch result.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which enzyme breaks down starch? (a) protease (b) lipase (c) amylase (d) bile
practice.answer: 5 (c) amylase - amylase (a carbohydrase) breaks starch into sugar.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Where does most absorption of digested food take place? (a) mouth (b) stomach (c) small intestine (d) large intestine
practice.answer: 6 (c) small intestine - its villi absorb most digested food into the blood.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The wave-like squeezing that pushes food along the gut is called: (a) absorption (b) peristalsis (c) digestion (d) respiration
practice.answer: 7 (b) peristalsis - muscular waves push food along the gut.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The main job of the large intestine is to: (a) digest protein (b) absorb most water (c) make bile (d) chew food
practice.answer: 8 (b) absorb most water - the large intestine reabsorbs water, leaving solid waste.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: An enzyme is best described as a: (a) type of sugar (b) living cell (c) biological catalyst (d) mineral
practice.answer: 9 (c) biological catalyst - enzymes speed reactions without being used up and are not alive.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these is NOT digested by the body but keeps the gut moving? (a) starch (b) fibre (c) protein (d) fat
practice.answer: 10 (b) fibre - fibre is not digested but keeps the gut moving.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The strong acid in the stomach helps to: (a) cool the food (b) kill germs and digest protein (c) absorb glucose (d) make vitamins
practice.answer: 11 (b) kill germs and digest protein - stomach acid does both jobs.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Villi increase the small intestine's: (a) length only (b) surface area for absorption (c) acid level (d) temperature
practice.answer: 12 (b) surface area for absorption - more surface means faster absorption.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Testing Foods For Starch
activities.materials: Iodine solution (from a pharmacy; the type used to clean wounds works, but ask an adult).; Small pieces of several foods: cooked rice, canjeero or bread, raw potato, a slice of banana, a little cooking oil, a little sugar, a piece of mango.; A clean white plate or several small saucers.; A dropper…
steps: Wash your hands and lay out a small piece of each food in its own spot on the plate.
Add one or two drops of iodine solution onto each food.
Watch carefully for about 30 seconds. A blue-black colour means starch is present. If the iodine stays its normal brown-orange colour, there is no starch.
Record the colour for each food in the table below.
Observation Table

activities.title: The Fat Test With Paper
activities.materials: Plain paper (brown paper or a page of a notebook works well).; Small amounts of: cooking oil, ghee (subag) or butter, a groundnut (peanut), a piece of avocado, a drop of water (for comparison), a little sugar.; A pencil to label; a warm sunny spot or a windowsill.
steps: Draw and label a small circle on the paper for each food.
Rub a little of each food onto its circle. For water, put one drop.
Leave the paper in a warm, airy place for about 15 minutes so any water dries.
Hold the paper up to the light. A translucent (see-through) greasy spot that does NOT dry away means the food contains fat. The water spot will dry and leave no permanent mark.
Record your results.

activities.title: Modelling Mechanical Digestion And Surface Area
activities.materials: Two clear glasses of water at the same temperature.; Two equal amounts of sugar (or two sugar cubes if you have them): one left whole/in a lump, one crushed or ground.; A spoon; a clock or phone to time.
steps: Fill both glasses with the same amount of water at the same temperature.
Into glass A, add the whole lump of sugar. Into glass B, add the crushed sugar. Do not stir.
Watch and time how long each takes to dissolve completely.
Record the times.
Observation Table

activities.title: Planning And Investigating A Balanced Plate
activities.materials: A notebook and pencil.; Access to observe (with permission) the meals eaten in your home for one day.; The nutrient list from your Lesson file.
steps: For one day, write down every food eaten at each meal in your home.
For each food, decide which nutrient group(s) it mainly provides.
Fill in the record sheet below, ticking which nutrients appeared.
Look for any nutrient group that was missing or eaten very little.
Design an improved balanced plate that fills the gap using local, affordable foods.

terms: A substance in food the body needs to live, grow and stay healthy.
A diet with the right amounts of all the nutrients.
Energy nutrient; includes starch and sugars (rice, canjeero, dates).
Growth and repair nutrient (fish, meat, beans, milk).
Energy store and warmth (oil, ghee, nuts).
Needed in tiny amounts to keep the body working (vitamin C, A, D).
Simple nutrient for bones and blood (calcium, iron).
Undigested plant material that keeps the gut moving.
Breaking food into small, soluble molecules.
Breaking molecules using enzymes and acid.
A protein that speeds up digestion; a biological catalyst.
commonMistakes: It ignores the mouth and small intestine.
The stomach mainly churns and starts protein digestion.
Enzymes are not cells or organisms.
It is not absorbed, but that is its value.

realProblems.prompt: Amina's mother plans a lunch of bariis and a cup of sweet tea. Which nutrient groups are missing, and what local foods could she add to balance it?
realProblems.answer: 1. Missing: protein, vitamins, minerals, fibre (and the tea adds sugar). Add fish, meat or beans (protein) and vegetables or fruit like mango (vitamins, minerals, fibre) to balance it.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1. Missing: protein, vitamins, minerals, fibre (and the tea adds sugar). Add fish, meat or beans (protein) and vegetables or fruit like mango (vitamins, minerals, fibre) to balance it.

realProblems.prompt: During Ramadan, Yusuf wants a suhoor that keeps him full and strong all day. Suggest a suitable meal and explain your choices using the idea of slow-energy foods.
realProblems.answer: 2. A good suhoor: canjeero or whole grains with beans, an egg or milk, and some fruit, plus water. These release energy slowly and keep Yusuf full and hydrated through the fasting day, unlike sugary foods that give a quick burst then fade.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 2. A good suhoor: canjeero or whole grains with beans, an egg or milk, and some fruit, plus water. These release energy slowly and keep Yusuf full and hydrated through the fasting day, unlike sugary foods that give a quick burst then fade.

realProblems.prompt: A family boils their drinking water before use. Explain how this protects the digestive system.
realProblems.answer: 3. Boiling water kills the harmful germs (bacteria) that cause diseases such as cholera and diarrhoea, protecting the gut and preventing dehydration and illness.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 3. Boiling water kills the harmful germs (bacteria) that cause diseases such as cholera and diarrhoea, protecting the gut and preventing dehydration and illness.

realProblems.prompt: Hassan chews a piece of canjeero for a long time and it tastes sweet. Explain why, naming the enzyme and the change.
realProblems.answer: 4. The enzyme amylase in saliva breaks the starch in the canjeero into sugar, which tastes sweet. This is chemical digestion beginning in the mouth.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 4. The enzyme amylase in saliva breaks the starch in the canjeero into sugar, which tastes sweet. This is chemical digestion beginning in the mouth.

realProblems.prompt: A young child in the community is tired and pale. Which mineral might be lacking, which foods would help, and what is the condition called?
realProblems.answer: 5. The child may lack the mineral iron; iron-rich foods such as red meat, liver, beans and dark green leaves would help. The condition of tiredness and paleness from too little iron is called anaemia.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 5. The child may lack the mineral iron; iron-rich foods such as red meat, liver, beans and dark green leaves would help. The condition of tiredness and paleness from too little iron is called anaemia.

realProblems.prompt: Explain, step by step, how a mouthful of fish is digested and absorbed, naming the organs, the enzyme group involved, and the final products.
realProblems.answer: 1. In the mouth the fish is chewed (mechanical). It passes down the oesophagus by peristalsis to the stomach, where acid and protease begin digesting the protein and germs are killed. In the small intestine, protease (with juices from the pancreas) finishes breaking the protein into amino acids.…
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1. In the mouth the fish is chewed (mechanical). It passes down the oesophagus by peristalsis to the stomach, where acid and protease begin digesting the protein and germs are killed. In the small intestine, protease (with juices from the pancreas) finishes breaking the protein into amino acids.…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the seven types of nutrient the body needs.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1. Carbohydrates, proteins, fats (lipids), vitamins, minerals, water, and fibre.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: State the main job of proteins in the body and give one local source.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2. Proteins build and repair body cells and tissues (growth). Local source: fish, goat meat, camel milk, or beans.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the difference between mechanical and chemical digestion in one sentence each.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3. Mechanical digestion physically breaks food into smaller pieces (e.g. chewing). Chemical digestion breaks large molecules into small ones using enzymes (e.g. amylase turning starch to sugar).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: List, in order, the five main organs food passes through from the mouth to the anus.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4. Mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine (then out through the anus).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is an enzyme? Why is it called a biological catalyst?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 5. An enzyme is a special protein that speeds up the breaking down of food. It is a biological catalyst because it speeds up a reaction without being used up itself.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain what 'absorption' means and where it mainly happens.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 6. Absorption is the passing of digested food from the gut into the blood. It happens mainly in the small intestine through the villi.

questions.question: Which nutrient is the body's main and fastest source of energy?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: carbohydrate - carbohydrates give the fastest, main energy supply.

questions.question: Which food is the best source of protein for growth?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: fish - fish is rich in protein for growth and repair; the others are mainly energy or empty.

questions.question: Iodine solution is used to test for:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: starch - iodine is the standard test for starch.

questions.question: A blue-black colour with iodine shows the presence of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: starch - the blue-black colour is the positive starch result.

questions.question: Which enzyme breaks down starch?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: amylase - amylase (a carbohydrase) breaks starch into sugar.

questions.question: Where does most absorption of digested food take place?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: small intestine - its villi absorb most digested food into the blood.

questions.question: The wave-like squeezing that pushes food along the gut is called:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: peristalsis - muscular waves push food along the gut.

questions.question: The main job of the large intestine is to:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: absorb most water - the large intestine reabsorbs water, leaving solid waste.

questions.question: An enzyme is best described as a:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: biological catalyst - enzymes speed reactions without being used up and are not alive.

questions.question: Which of these is NOT digested by the body but keeps the gut moving?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: fibre - fibre is not digested but keeps the gut moving.

questions.question: The strong acid in the stomach helps to:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: kill germs and digest protein - stomach acid does both jobs.

questions.question: Villi increase the small intestine's:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 7 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: surface area for absorption - more surface means faster absorption.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you will be able to:
I can name the main food groups and nutrients - carbohydrates, proteins, fats (lipids), vitamins, minerals, water and fibre - and give a local food example of each.
I can explain the job of each nutrient in the body and describe what happens when a nutrient is missing (deficiency).
I can describe a balanced diet and explain why different people (a farmer, a baby, a pregnant mother, a person fasting) need different amounts of food.
I can name the organs of the digestive system in order and state the function of each: mouth, oesophagus, stomach, small intestine, large intestine, and the helper organs (liver, pancreas).
I can explain the difference between mechanical and chemical digestion and give an example of each.
I can describe what enzymes are and explain how they break large food molecules into small, soluble ones.
I can explain absorption - how digested food passes into the blood through the wall of the small intestine.
